site.name: Game Over Gear
site.tagline: Retro gaming gear, by the way you play.

niche.subcategory: retro consoles & systems (reissues, FPGA, minis) + classic-console, arcade & emulation hardware

voice.tone: playful and nostalgic about the 8-bit/16-bit and arcade era, but spec-precise; talks to the grown-up who grew up on a CRT, never gatekeeps the hobby
voice.core_anxiety: spending money on retro gear that feels wrong, lags, or isn't compatible with the original hardware
framings: Authenticity: how close does it get to the original feel/look
Compatibility: what consoles, regions, and displays it actually works with
Modern-convenience vs purist tradeoffs (HDMI/scalers vs real CRT)
Buy-once vs cheap-and-replace for controllers and cables

default.name: Authenticity
default.description: how close to the original feel, latency, and look

default.name: Compatibility
default.description: consoles, regions, displays, and media it supports

default.name: Value
default.description: performance per dollar, buy-once vs replace

pillars.label: Consoles & Systems
pillars.blurb: Reissue consoles, FPGA systems, and minis: NEOGEO AES, Analogue, Hyperkin, and more.

pillars.label: Controllers
pillars.blurb: Wired and wireless pads that feel like the originals.

pillars.label: Arcade Sticks
pillars.blurb: Fight sticks and cabinet parts for the coin-op feel.

pillars.label: Handhelds
pillars.blurb: Emulation handhelds from pocket to powerhouse.

pillars.label: Displays & Scalers
pillars.blurb: CRTs, RetroTINK, OSSC, and the path to a clean picture.

pillars.label: Level Up
pillars.blurb: Setup guides, region-lock explainers, and the why behind the gear.

pillars.label: Gifts & 1-Ups
pillars.blurb: Nostalgic gifts and fun gear for the retro-obsessed.

doctrineNotes: Comparison-and-fit framework. Never claim hands-on use; cite manufacturer specs, verified-buyer reviews, and independent testing (My Life in Gaming, RetroRGB, Modern Vintage Gamer) as third-party evidence. No em dashes. No defensive audience exclusions. Visual identity is retro 8-bit/16-bit + Atari + arcade-cabinet nostalgia (pixel display type, CRT scanlines, neon arcade palette); body copy stays readable, the retro lives in the chrome.
